title: Prickly heat and summer rashes
excerpt: Prickly heat, known in Ayurveda as udarda or pittaja tvak vikara, is a sign that your body's internal heat is overflowing. A few cooling habits drawn from classical texts can bring real relief through the summer months.
body_html:
when the skin starts to burn in summer

You know the feeling: the moment you step outside in May or June, tiny red bumps appear across your chest, back, and neck. They prick, they itch, and no amount of fanning seems to help. In India this is simply called ghamoriyan, the rash that comes with sweat. Doctors call it miliaria. Ayurveda has been describing this condition for more than two thousand years, and the classical granthas offer a surprisingly clear picture of what is happening inside the body.

The Ayurvedic term most closely linked to heat rashes is udarda, a skin condition marked by raised, burning eruptions that worsen in hot weather. The texts also describe it within the broader group of pittaja tvak vikaras, disorders of the skin that arise when pitta dosha, the body's fire-and-water principle, becomes excessive and disturbs the sweat channels. In the Sushruta Samhita, these channels are called svedavaha srotas, and their obstruction is understood to drive many summer skin complaints (Sushruta Samhita, Nidanasthana).

When pitta climbs beyond what the body can manage, it accumulates in the skin and thickens or blocks the sweat channels.

the ayurvedic view: pitta, sweat, and blocked channels

Pitta governs body heat, digestion, and skin colour. In summer, the sun naturally increases pitta in everyone. When pitta climbs beyond what the body can manage, it accumulates in the skin and thickens or blocks the sweat channels. The result is exactly what you feel: a burning, prickling sensation under the skin, red papules, and sometimes a generalised irritability that classical authors connect to pitta's psychological dimension.

The Ashtanga Hridaya, one of the most practical of the classical texts, recommends a summer regimen called grishma ritucharya. It advises cool baths, light and moist foods, and avoiding direct afternoon sun precisely because these habits prevent pitta from going out of balance in the first place (Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana, Ch. 3). The Bhavaprakasha similarly notes that heating foods, physical labour in the afternoon heat, and suppressing natural urges such as thirst or sweating all aggravate pitta in the skin (Bhavaprakasha, Purva Khanda). The logic is consistent: anything that adds heat when the body is already warm tips the scale toward rash and inflammation.

gentle everyday habits that help

The good news is that the classical recommendations for cooling pitta are simple, inexpensive, and well suited to an Indian home. The tradition of applying chandana, sandalwood paste, to the skin is not merely cosmetic. Sandalwood is classified as a sheet virya herb, one with a cooling energy, and applying a thin paste of sandalwood powder mixed with rose water to affected areas is a time-honoured way to reduce localised burning. Multani mitti, fuller's earth, is another traditional topical remedy for exactly this kind of heat rash: it absorbs excess heat and sweat from the skin's surface.

Inside the body, what you eat and drink matters during summer. Classical texts favour drinks made with tender coconut water, cooled cumin water, or coriander-seed infusions. Cumin and coriander are classified in Ayurvedic nighantu texts, including the Bhavaprakasha Nighantu, as herbs that balance pitta and support gentle detoxification. Fennel, too, is considered cooling and is easy to sip as a simple tea or chew as seeds after a meal. Avoiding very spicy food, fried snacks, and excessive salt during the hottest months is a practical way to keep pitta from climbing.

In terms of daily routine, a lukewarm or cool bath with a small amount of neem leaves steeped in the water, or with a handful of sandalwood powder added, can soothe the skin and reduce bacterial overgrowth that often complicates sweat rashes. Loose, breathable cotton clothing is strongly implied by the classical emphasis on avoiding heat-trapping environments. The Ashtanga Hridaya advises resting in the shade of trees or cool rooms during the afternoon (Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana, Ch. 3), which translates in modern life to avoiding the outdoors between noon and four when the sun is at its peak.

when to see a doctor

This article is general information and is not a diagnosis or a substitute for medical care. Most cases of prickly heat resolve on their own with cooling measures and improved ventilation. However, you should consult a qualified doctor or vaidya if: the rash spreads rapidly or covers large areas of the body; the skin becomes very swollen, weeping, or develops pus-filled blisters; you develop a fever alongside the rash; the rash does not improve within a week despite home care; or you notice signs of heat exhaustion such as dizziness, rapid heartbeat, or confusion. Children, elderly people, and anyone with a compromised immune system should seek professional guidance early rather than waiting.

Try This Today

  • Sandalwood cooling paste: Mix two teaspoons of pure sandalwood powder with enough rose water to form a thin paste. Apply to the affected area, let it sit for 20 minutes, then rinse with cool water. Do this once daily during a flare.
  • Multani mitti pack: Mix multani mitti with cool water or rose water into a smooth paste and apply to the rash. Leave for 15 minutes or until it begins to dry, then rinse gently. It absorbs surface heat and soothes itching.
  • Cumin or coriander cooling water: Boil one teaspoon of cumin seeds or coriander seeds in two cups of water for five minutes. Cool, strain, and sip throughout the day. Both are considered pitta-balancing in the classical tradition.
  • Neem-leaf bath: Steep a handful of fresh or dried neem leaves in warm water for ten minutes and use that water to bathe. Neem has long been used in the classical tradition as an antiseptic and anti-inflammatory herb for skin.
  • Avoid the afternoon sun: Classical summer regimen texts advise resting indoors between noon and four o'clock. In practical terms this means scheduling outdoor exercise for early morning or after sunset during the hot months.

Key Ayurvedic Ingredients

Cumin (जीरा)

Classified as pitta-balancing and gently detoxifying; cumin water is a classical summer drink

Coriander (धनिया)

Cooling in potency, used in infusions to pacify pitta and support the skin in summer

Fennel (सौंफ)

Sweet and cooling; chewed as seeds or sipped as tea to reduce internal heat
author: Kerala Ayurveda Editorial Team

metafields.dek: Prickly heat, known in Ayurveda as udarda or pittaja tvak vikara, is a sign that your body's internal heat is overflowing. A few cooling habits drawn from classical texts can bring real relief through the summer months.
metafields.disclaimer: Ayurveda guidance on AyurHQ is supportive care, not emergency or diagnostic medical treatment.
at_a_glance: Prickly heat is linked in Ayurveda to excess pitta blocking the sweat channels, especially in summer
Cooling applications like sandalwood paste and multani mitti are classical remedies for the burning and prickling
A summer diet that avoids spicy, fried food and includes cooling drinks like cumin water and coconut water helps prevent recurrence

glossary_terms.definition: A classical Ayurvedic skin condition marked by burning, raised eruptions, often associated with excess pitta
Skin disorders arising from imbalanced pitta dosha, characterised by heat, redness, and inflammation
The channels in the body that carry sweat; their blockage is linked to heat rashes in classical texts
The seasonal regimen prescribed for summer in Ayurvedic texts, focused on cooling the body and pacifying pitta
Cooling in potency; a quality attributed to herbs and foods that reduce heat and calm pitta
Sandalwood; a classical cooling herb used topically to relieve burning sensations and skin inflammation

sources: Sushruta Samhita, Sharirasthana and Chikitsasthana (skin disease and srotas framework)

faqs.question: What is the meaning of "Udarda" in Ayurveda?
faqs.answer: In the Ayurvedic tradition, Udarda is defined as: A classical Ayurvedic skin condition marked by burning, raised eruptions, often associated with excess pitta

faqs.question: How can I start addressing this issue today?
faqs.answer: One of the most recommended practices is to sandalwood cooling paste. Mix two teaspoons of pure sandalwood powder with enough rose water to form a thin paste. Apply to the affected area, let it sit for 20 minutes, then rinse with cool water. Do this once daily during a flare.

faqs.question: What is the benefit of Cumin for this condition?
faqs.answer: According to classical texts, Cumin is highly regarded. Classified as pitta-balancing and gently detoxifying; cumin water is a classical summer drink
